A Constitution lays out certain ideals that form the basis of the kind of country a nation aspires to be.
*Tradition of Constitution began in:* **USA (in 1787)**.
Independence of USA: 4th July 1776.
While all democratic countries are likely to have a Constitution, it is not necessary that all countries that have a Constitution are democratic.
**Democratic Government:** A democratic government is a form of government in which power is held by the people and exercised through their elected representatives.
*Communist:* Source or means of production controlled by government. E.g. **China**.
*Oligarchic:* A government ruled by a few. E.g. **China, Russia**.
*Monarchic:* A government by a single person specifically King or a monarch. E.g. **Saudi Arabia, UK**.
*Totalitarian:* It refers to total control over lives of citizens by whoever is ruling E.g. **North Korea**.
**1928:** The early draft of the Indian Constitution (Nehru Report) was submitted by the All Parties Conference at Lucknow chaired by **Motilal Nehru**.
**1934:** M. N. Roy first proposed the idea of a Constituent Assembly for India.
**1935:** The Indian National Congress officially demanded a Constitutional Assembly.

**1938:** Jawaharlal Nehru, on behalf of the INC, declared that 'the Constitution of free India must be framed without outside interference, by a Constituent Assembly elected on the basis of adult franchise'.
*Reason:* Ongoing World War II (1939-1945)
August Offer: **1940**
Cripps Proposal: **1942**
Wavell Plan: **1945**

Cabinet Mission Plan: **1946** → with 3 British Cabinet members: **Pethick-Lawrence (chairman), Stafford Cripps, and A.V. Alexander.** Only Cabinet Mission Plan was accepted by both the Congress and the Muslim League, hence Constituent Assembly was formed according to it.

items: **Schedule 7:** 3 lists containing subject in which Union and States can make laws. (Federal Provisions) **Union list, State list, Concurrent list**

__Union List Subjects:__ Initially 97, presently 100 subjects (Defence, arms and ammunition, atomic energy, citizenship, airways, inter-state trade and commerce, banking, foreign affairs, labour and mines, census, etc.)

__State List Subjects:__ Initially 66, presently 61 subjects. (Public order, local government, health, pilgrimage, liquor, agriculture, fisheries, betting and gambling, Co-operative societies, Land, Lotteries).

blocks.text: Five scenarios in which Parliament/Union can legislate on State list subjects-

items: 1) National Emergency (Article 250): Law valid 6 months after emergency ends.
2) President's Rule (Article 356): Law continues until repealed/amended later or if LS passes a resolution through Simple Majority.
3) If two or more state legislature requests (Article 252): Permanent until repealed by parliament, states cannot amend it themselves.
4) To enforce international treaty (Article 253).
5) If Rajya Sabha gives Parliament this power (by special majority) in National Interest (Article 249): A resolution thus passed is valid for one year + 6 months after expiry.

__Concurrent List Subjects:__ Initially 47, presently 52 subjects. Both State and Centre can make laws on subjects given in this list. But if both make law Centre's law will prevail over States' law. States' law can prevail only in 1 case when the States' Bill is given assent by President. (Marriage and divorce, criminal laws, code of criminal procedure, insolvency and bankruptcy, trade and labour disputes, electricity, education, weights and measures, etc.)
Parliament can later nullify such a state law by passing another law on the same subject.

blocks.text:
Residuary List:
• These subjects are not included in Union/State/Concurrent list.
• Parliament has the power to legislate on residuary subjects.

blocks.caption: Image of the Preamble of the Constitution of India.

items: This shows that the constitution has been drawn up and enacted by the people through their representatives, and not handed down to them by a king or any outside powers.
People have supreme right to make decisions on internal as well as external matters. **No external power can dictate** the government of India.
Wealth is generated socially and should be **shared equally** by society.
Government should regulate the ownership of land and industry to **reduce socio-economic inequalities**.
Example: Russia
→ **Capitalistic: USA**
→ **Mixed: India**
The term 'Socialism' derives its inspiration from the Russian Revolution.
Types of Socialism:
**Radical Socialism:** The idea of ending private ownership of businesses and wealth and making everything shared so that everyone is equal (Classless Society).
**Gandhian Socialism:** A system promoting Non-violence, Self-reliance and fair wealth distribution through village-centered economy.
**Nehruism Socialism:** A system emphasizing state-controlled development to achieve economic growth and social equality. It is also known as Mixed Socialism.
Citizens have complete **freedom to follow any religion or no religion at all**. There is no official state religion.
Government treats all religious beliefs and practices equally and impartially.
In India, secularism follows a positive concept. (Involvement of state in religious matters).
The government functions according to a set of constitutional rules and principles.
**A Government Of the People, By the People, For the People-As said by Abraham Lincoln**
Democracy means a system of 'Government by consent'- **John Locke** (Father of Liberalism). Book- 'Two Treatises of Government' 1689.

**Direct Democracy:** A form of government in which citizens directly participate in the decision-making process and vote on laws and policies themselves. (Eg:- Switzerland, Uruguay, Latvia, Lithuania etc).
**Indirect Democracy (Representative Democracy):** A form of government in which citizens elect representatives who make laws and take decisions on their behalf. (Eg:- Canada, India, USA, UK, etc).

Is Preamble a Part of Constitution and is it Amendable?

items: Supreme Court held that-
i) The Preamble is **not a part** of the Constitution & hence cannot be amended.
ii) It is just a key to understanding the Constitution.
iii) It is **not legally enforceable**.
In this case, Supreme Court Overruled the Berubari view and held that –
i) **The Preamble is a part of the Constitution and hence can be amended under Article 368.**
ii) It reflects the **basic structure** of the Constitution (decided by the Supreme Court).
i) Reaffirmed the Kesavananda Bharati case ruling.

items: It is not enforceable in the Court of Law.

blocks.text: PART I - UNION AND ITS TERRITORY [Article 1-4]
**Article 1: Name and Territory of the Union**

items: **1(1)** India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of States.
**1(2)** The States and the territories, thereof, shall be as specified in the First Schedule.
**1(3)** The territory of India shall comprise of-
(a) The territories of the States;
(b) The Union territories specified in the First Schedule; and
(c) Such other territories as may be acquired in future.

blocks.text:
__Type of Polity__
- India is not a Federation but a Union of States.
- Term 'federation' has nowhere been mentioned in the Constitution.
- **Federation of State:** A new entity that is formed through a treaty or an agreement between various federating units. **E.g. USA**
- **Union of States:** No agreement between the Federating units (States). They do not have a right to secede from the Union. **Eg. India**
- India is an Indestructible Union of Destructible states.
- US is an indestructible union of indestructible states.
**Article 2: Admission or Establishment of new States**

items: Parliament may by law admit into the Union, or establish, new states on such terms and conditions as it thinks fit.

blocks.text: **Article 3: Formation of new States and alteration of areas, boundaries, or names of existing States**

items: Parliament may by law—
(a) form a new State by separation of territory from any State or by uniting two or more States or parts of States or by uniting any territory to a part of any State;
(b) increase the area of any State;
(c) diminish the area of any State;
(d) alter the boundaries of any State;
(e) alter the name of any State

blocks.text:
**NOTE:** Bill related to subject matters given in Article 3 shall be introduced with the prior recommendation of the President, who shall refer this bill to the legislature of the state concerned.
- 5th Constitutional amendment empowered the President to set a time limit for state legislature to express its views.
Difference between Article 2 and 3: Article 2 talks about incorporating foreign territory into India while Article 3 talks about the changes in the existing states.
**Article 4: Laws made under Article 2 and 3 to provide for the amendment of first and fourth Schedules**

items: **4(2)** Any law made under Article 2 or Article 3 that amends the First or Fourth Schedule of the Constitution shall not be considered a constitutional amendment under Article 368. Therefore, such a law can **be passed by a simple majority in Parliament.**
If Indian territory is to be ceded, then amendment is needed under Article 368. **(Berubari Union case, 1960)**

items: The State Reorganisation Act of 1956 was passed based on the recommendations of the Fazl Ali Commission.
It removed the fourfold classification of states and created 14 States + 6 Union Territories, on 1st Nov, 1956
With regard to North India, it sought to divide the huge Hindi speaking belt into four states: Bihar, UP, MP, and Rajasthan.
In the east, the existing provinces could stay as they were with minor adjustments.
The commission rejected the demand for tribal states to be carved out of Bihar and Assam.
The commission did not agree with the creation of a Sikh state.

blocks.text: **Article 5: Citizenship at the commencement of the Constitution**

items: A person shall be deemed to be a citizen of India on 26 January 1950, if they fulfill any of the following condition-
(a) Born in India
(b) Either of the parents was born in India or,
(c) Has been an ordinary resident of India for at least 5 years immediately before 26 January, 1950
**Article 6:** Persons who migrated from Pakistan to India before 19 July 1948 were granted citizenship automatically.

**Article 7:** Persons who migrated from India to Pakistan after 1st March 1947 were not considered citizens of India, unless they returned to India with a permit for resettlement or permanent return.
**Article 8:** Persons of Indian origin residing outside India may acquire Indian citizenship by registering at the Indian embassy in their country of residence.
**Article 9:** Provided that if any person voluntarily acquired the citizenship of a foreign State, he/she will no longer be a citizen of India.
**Article 10:** Continuance of rights of citizenship.
**Article 11:** Parliament to regulate the right of citizenship by law.
Deals with the provisions of Citizenship **after 26 Jan, 1950**.
**Aquisition of Citizenship –**
**1. By Birth (sec-3):**
**Jus Soli (Right of Soil):** Before 1 July 1987, any person born in India automatically acquired Indian citizenship, irrespective of the nationality of the parents.

**Jus Sanguinis (Right of Blood):** Due to concerns regarding illegal immigration, the law was amended. For persons born after 3 December 2003, citizenship by birth is granted only if at least one parent is an Indian citizen and the other is not an illegal immigrant at the time of birth.

**2. By Descent (sec-4):** A person born outside India can acquire Indian citizenship by descent if either of the parents is an Indian citizen and the birth is registered at the Indian consulate within one year of birth.
**3. By registration (sec-5):** granted to Indian-origin individuals, spouses of Indian citizens, or minor children who have resided in India for 7 years.
**4. By Naturalisation (sec-6):** granted to foreign nationals who have been –
Residing in India for 12 years (11 years in the preceding 14 years + 1 year immediately before application).
They must know one Indian Language mentioned in the 8th schedule.
Third Schedule of Citizenship Act 1955, mentions qualifications for Naturalisation.

blocks.text: **Note:** The Citizenship (Amendment) Act, 2003 provides that an illegal migrant cannot acquire Indian citizenship through registration or naturalization. It also laid the foundation for the creation of the National Register of Citizens (NRC).

items: **5. By incorporating a territory (sec-7):** When a new territory becomes part of India, the Government of India specifies which persons of that territory shall be granted Indian citizenship.

blocks.text: Fundamental rights are a set of legally enforceable rights.

items: Fundamental Rights –
are not absolute. (but have limits)
are not 'Sacrosanct' or Permanent.
can be curtailed during emergency (but **Article 20 and 21 can never be suspended**).
are justiciable in nature. (One can approach the court if they are violated.)
They are borrowed from the Constitution of USA. (Bill of Rights).
They are contained in **Part III** of the Constitution → **Article (12-35)**
They are termed as **"Conscience of Constitution"**
They seek to implement **political democracy**.
They are referred to as the **"Magna Carta of India"**

Originally there were **7** fundamental rights, but currently there are 6 Fundamental Rights (Right to property was removed from **Part III in 1978 by 44th CA, It is now a legal right under Article 300-A, Part XII of the constitution**)
**Doctrine of Waiver:** It means voluntarily giving up a known legal right or claim.
Six Fundamental Rights given in the constitution are:
→ **Right to Equality: Article 14-18**
→ **Right to Freedom: Article 19-22**
→ **Right against Exploitation: Article 23-24**
→ **Right to Freedom of Religion: Article 25-28**
→ **Cultural and Educational Rights: Article 29-30**
→ **Right to Constitutional Remedies: Article 32**

It defines the term "State" mentioned in part III which includes the Government and Parliament of India and the Government and the Legislature of each of the States and all local or other authorities within the territory of India or under the control of the Government of India.

Article 13: Laws Inconsistent with or in Derogation of the Fundamental Rights

items: **Article 13(1):** Any law made before the commencement of constitution shall be declared null and void by judiciary if it violates Fundamental Rights.
**Doctrine of Eclipse: based upon 13(1):** A law that violates Fundamental Rights becomes inoperative to that extent but is not completely void.
**Article 13(2):** Any law made after the Constitution that violates Fundamental Rights is void to the extent of the violation.
**Article 13(3):** Law includes any ordinance, order, by-law, rule, regulation, notification, in the territory of India.
**Article 13(4):** This article shall not apply to any amendment made under Article 368.

Article 15: Prohibition of Discrimination on Grounds of Religion, Race, Caste, Sex or Place of Birth

items: **15(1)** The State shall not discriminate against any citizen on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth.
**15(2)** No citizen shall, on these grounds, be subject to any disability, liability, restriction or condition with regard to-
(a) Access to shops, public restaurants, hotels and places of public entertainment; or
(b) The use of wells, tanks, bathing ghats, roads and places of public resort maintained wholly or partly out of State funds.
**15(3)** the State can make special provisions for **women and children**, e.g. few Metro seats are reserved for women.
**15(4)** the State can make special provision for the **advancement of Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes.** Added through Champakam Dorairajan case which lead to 1st CA(1951).

**15(5)** Inserted by the 93rd CA 2005, it allows the State to make special provisions, including reservations, for socially and educationally backward classes, SCs and STs in educational institutions.
**15(6)** Added by the 103rd CA 2019, it allows the State to provide up to 10% reservation for Economically Weaker Sections (EWS).

blocks.text: Article 16: Equality of Opportunity in Matters of Public Employment

items: **16(1)** Equality of opportunity for all citizens in matters relating to **employment or appointment** to any office under the state.

**16(2)** No citizen shall, on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, descent, place of birth, residence, be ineligible for, or discriminated against in respect of, **any employment or office under the state.**
**16(3)** Parliament can set rule that gives **preference to people who are residents** of the area when it comes to local employment or appointments.
**16(4)** State can make rules to reserve jobs or positions for **"backward classes"** who are underrepresented in the state services.

**16(4A)** Introduced by the 77th CA 1995, it empowers the State to provide reservation in promotion with consequential seniority for SCs and STs in state services if they are not adequately represented.
**16(4B)** Introduced by the 81st CA 2000, it allows the carry forward of unfilled reserved vacancies (Doctrine of advancement) to subsequent years.
**16(5)** Allows religious institutions such as temples, mosque, churches to require that their employees belong to the same religion.
**16(6)** Introduced by the 103rd CA 2019 , it permits up to 10% reservation for Economically Weaker Sections (EWS) in public employment.

blocks.text: Article 22: Protection against Arrest & Detention in certain cases

items: → A person being arrested under ordinary law has:
**22(1)** Gives the right to consult and be defended by a legal practitioner of one's choice.
**22(2)** An arrested person must be produced before the nearest magistrate within 24 hours (excluding travel time).
**22(3)** The protections of clauses (1) and (2) do not apply to enemy aliens and persons under preventive detention.
**22(4)** Preventive detention cannot exceed three months unless approved by an Advisory Board.
**22(5)** The grounds of detention must be communicated to the detenu and an opportunity to make a representation must be given.
**22(6)** Authorities may withhold facts if disclosure is against public interest.
**22(7)** Parliament may prescribe the circumstances and maximum period of preventive detention.

Judicial custody means an accused is detained in jail under the supervision of a Magistrate, rather than in police lock-up, following a court order. blocks.text: Article 32: Remedies for enforcement of rights conferred by this part

items: **32(1)** Right to approach the Supreme Court for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights.

blocks.text: Cases related to DPSPs vs Fundamental Rights (FRs)
Champakam Dorairajan Case (1951): Supreme Court ruled that—

items: FRs would prevail over DPSP.
FRs are amendable.

blocks.text: Golaknath Case (1967): Parliament cannot revoke/amend FRs.

items: In response to the Golaknath case, the Parliament enacted 24th amendment which empowered Parliament to amend FRs.
**25th Amendment:** Inserted Article 31(C)
(i) Laws implementing DPSP under Articles 39(b) and 39(c) cannot be challenged on the grounds of contravention of Article 14 & 19.
(ii) No such law can be questioned in the court.

blocks.text: Keshavananda Bharti Case (1973): A 13 judges bench ruled that-

items: Parliament can amend FRs but cannot disturb the Basic Structure of the Constitution.
The 2nd provision of the Article 31(C) which restricted judicial reviews of laws was declared unconstitutional.

blocks.text: Minerva Mill Case (1980): Indian Constitution is found on the bedrock of balance between FRs and DPSP.
Elements of the Basic structure of the Constitution

items: 1. Separation of Power
2. Unity & Integrity of the Nation
3. Fundamental Rights
4. Judicial Review
5. Supremacy of the Constitution
6. Parliamentary System of Government

rows: Automatically enforceable, Court can invalidate laws violating FRs.
Require legislative enactment, Courts cannot invalidate laws violating DPSP.

items: FDs are borrowed from the **USSR**.
Statutory and non-enforceable / Non-justiciable
Not originally part of the Constitution.
Introduced through the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act (1976)
Applicable only to Indian citizens, not foreigners.

blocks.text: Committee that suggested addition of Fundamental Duties:

items: *Swaran Singh Committee* proposed Fundamental Duties in 1976.
Recommended 8 duties. *(Prime Minister - Indira Gandhi)*
5 duties accepted, 3 duties rejected.
**Rejected duties:** Imposing penalties for not observing duties. / Prohibition against challenging penalty laws in courts. / Duty to pay taxes.
On the recommendations of *Swaran Singh Committee the 42nd CA of 1976* was made to *add 10 FDs* in Part IVA & Article 51 A.
The 11th Fundamental Duty *[Article 51A(k)]* was added later via *86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002.*
(a) to abide by the Constitution and **respect** its ideals and institutions, **the National Flag and the National Anthem**;
(b) to cherish and **follow the noble ideals** that inspired the national struggle for freedom;
(c) to uphold and protect the **sovereignty, unity and integrity** of India;
(d) to **defend** the country and render national service when called upon to do so;

(e) to promote harmony and the spirit of **common brotherhood** among all the people of India, transcending religious, linguistic and regional or sectional diversities; to renounce practices derogatory to the **dignity of women**;
(f) to value and preserve the rich **heritage** of our composite culture;
(g) to protect and improve the natural **environment** including **forests**, lakes, rivers and wildlife, and to have compassion for living creatures;
(h) to develop the **scientific temper, humanism** and the spirit of inquiry and reform;
(i) to safeguard public property and to **abjure violence**;
(j) to **strive towards excellence** in all spheres of individual and collective activity so that the nation constantly rises to higher levels of endeavour and achievement;
(k) duty of a parent or guardian: to provide opportunities for education to his child or ward between the age of six and fourteen years. *(Added through 86th CA, 2002)*
It identified the existence of legal provisions for implementation of Fundamental Duties and teaching school kids about their duties.

blocks.text: Laws Reflecting and Enforcing Fundamental Duties

items: *The Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act (1967)* — Prevents activities that threaten the sovereignty and integrity of India – *Duty (c)*
*Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act (1971)* — Prohibits disrespect to the Constitution, National Flag, and National Anthem. – *Duty (a)*
*Wildlife Protection Act (1972)* — Provides legal protection to wild animals and birds. – *Duty (g)*
*Forest Conservation Act (1980)* — Restricts deforestation and conserves forests. – *Duty (g)*
*Representation of the People Act (1951)* — Regulates elections and upholds democratic processes. – *Duty (a)*
*Indian Penal Code (IPC)* — Defines crimes and prescribes punishments, protecting public order and morality. – *Duties (i) and (e) (to renounce derogatory practices against women)*
*Protection of Civil Rights Act (1955)* — Eliminates untouchability and promotes equality. – *Duty (e)*

blocks.text: Fundamental duties are provided for Indian citizens only and not for Foreigners.

items: According to H. R. Gokhale (then Law Minister), Fundamental Duties were added in 1976 to control anti-national and unconstitutional activities that increased before the 1975 Emergency.
The Fundamental Duties partly resemble the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR).
The Constitution Review Commission (2002) chaired by M. N. Venkatachaliah recommended measures for effective implementation of Fundamental Duties.

items: Part V of the Constitution is divided into 5 chapters:
Chapter I: **Union Executive (President + VP + PM + COMs + Attorney General) (Art 52-78)**
Chapter II: **Parliament (Art 79-122)**
Chapter III: **Legislative powers of the President (Art 123)**
Chapter IV: **Union Judiciary (Art 124-147)**
Chapter V: **Comptroller and Auditor General of India (Art 148-151)**
**Article 52:** There shall be a President of India, who shall be the Head of the Union Executive
**Article 53:** Executive power of Union shall vest in President
He is the Supreme Commander of all the defence forces (Army, Navy, Airforce)
**Article 54:** Election of President

President is elected by an electoral college; Consisting of Elected members of both Houses of Parliament (Lok Sabha + Rajya Sabha) + Elected members of the Legislative Assemblies of the States + Elected members of the Legislative Assemblies of the UTs of Delhi and Puducherry.

blocks.text: **Note:** Nominated members of Parliament and Members of Legislative Council do not participate in the election of the President.

items: The President is:
Titular Head of the country.
First citizen of India (Ceremonial).
Nominal head / De jure head of the country.
**Article 55:** Manner of election
Indirect election (not directly by the people)
**Proportional representation + Single Transferable Vote (STV)**
Voting done by Secret ballot.
Security Deposit is ₹ 15,000

blocks.text:
**Vote Value Calculations:**
• President Elections— **50 proposers + 50 seconders**

• Value of vote of an MP = **Total value of votes of all MLAs / Total number of elected MPs.**
• Electoral quota = **(Total number of valid votes polled / Vacancies +1) +1**

items: **Article 72:** Pardoning power of the President (Judiciary Power)
**Types of Pardoning Power of President:**
1. **Pardon** – Completely removes punishment and conviction.
2. **Commutation** – Changes the form of punishment.
3. **Remission** – Reduces the punishment period.
4. **Respite** – Lessen the punishment due to special conditions. (e.g., pregnancy)
5. **Reprieve** – Stay on execution for a temporary duration.

blocks.text:
**Q.** What is the difference between the pardoning power of President and Governor?
**Ans:** The President can pardon death sentences and court-martial punishments while the Governor cannot. *(Pardoning power of Governor is given in Article 161)*
Maru Ram v. Union of India (1980):

items: SC held that the pardoning power of the President under Article 72 and the Governor under Article 161 is not an absolute or personal power, but must be exercised on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers and is subject to limited judicial review on grounds such as mala fide, arbitrariness, or extraneous considerations.

blocks.text: Kehar Singh vs Union of India Case (1988):

items: Pardoning power of the President is not subject to judicial review, unless the decision is arbitrary, mala fide, irrational, or discriminatory.

blocks.text: PRIME MINISTER AND COUNCIL OF MINISTERS (Article 74-78)
Article 74: Council of Ministers to aid and advise President

items: **74(1)** There shall be a Council of Ministers with the Prime Minister as the head to aid and advise the President who shall act in accordance with the advice of the Council of Ministers.
The PM is known as the **Real/ De facto** head of the government. (President is known as **Nominal / De jure** head.)

blocks.text:
The Prime Minister of India is the Chairman of the:
— NITI AAYOG
— Interstate Council
— National Disaster Management Authority

**42nd Constitutional Amendment** made the advise binding on President.
**44th Constitutional Amendment:** President may request the Council of Ministers to reconsider advice, however, after reconsideration, the President must ultimately act according to the advice tendered.

items: **74(2)** The question, whether any, and if so what advice was tendered by ministers to the President **shall not be inquired into by any court**.

blocks.text: Article 75: Other provisions as to Ministers

items: **75(1)** The Prime Minister shall be appointed by the President and other ministers shall be appointed by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister.
**75(1)(A)** The total number of Ministers, including the Prime Minister; **shall not exceed 15%** of the total strength of the Lok Sabha.
**75(1)(B)** If any member is disqualified on the grounds of defection, they cannot become minister.
**75(2)** Ministers hold office till the pleasure of the President
**75(3)** Council of Ministers is **collectively responsible** to the House of the People (Lok Sabha).
**75(4)** Before entering office, the Prime Minister and Ministers must take oaths of office and secrecy administered by the President.
**75(5)** A Minister who is not a member of either Houses of the Parliament for **six consecutive months** shall cease to be a Minister at the end of that period.
**75(6)** Salaries and allowances of Ministers are determined by Parliament and until Parliament fixes that, it shall be as specified in the 2nd Schedule.
**Cabinet Ministers:** Important portfolios like Defence, Home, Finance, Education, Health. Participate in Cabinet meetings.
**Minister of State:** Handles smaller ministries (Minister of Independent charge) or assist Cabinet Ministers (Ministers attached to ministry). Do not participate in Cabinet meetings.
**Deputy Ministers:** Assist Cabinet Ministers and Ministers of State.

**76(4)** Attorney General holds office till the pleasure of the President and receives remuneration as determined by the President.
He is not a full-time counsel of the government.

blocks.text: Article 77: Conduct of Business of the Government of India

items: **77(1)** All executive actions of Government of India shall be expressed to be taken in the name of the President.
**77(2)** Orders and instruments made in the name of the President shall be authenticated in such manner as specified in rules made by the President.
**77(3)** The President shall make rules for more convenient transaction of the business of Government of India and allocate responsibilities among Ministers.

**78(b)** To furnish such information relating to the administration of the Union and proposals for legislation as the President may call for.

**78(c)** If the President so requires, to submit for the consideration of the Council of Ministers any matter on which a decision has been taken by a Minister but which has not been considered by the Council.

A Bill is a Money Bill if it contains only provisions related to:
(a) Imposition, abolition, remission, alteration, regulation of any tax.
(b) Regulation of borrowings.
(c) Custody and audit of Consolidated Fund or Contingency Fund
(d) Appropriation of money out of Consolidated Fund.
(e) Declaring any expenditure as charged on Consolidated Fund.
(f) The receipt of money into the Consolidated Fund of India or the Public Account of India, or the custody or issue of such money.

items: After a bill is passed by both the Houses the bill is then presented to the President.
President may:
(a) Give assent – Bill becomes a law.
(b) Use its Veto power.
*Absolute Veto:* President rejects the Bill completely.
→ This veto was first used by Dr. Rajendra Prasad for PEPSU Appropriation Bill 1954.
→ In 1991 R. Venkataraman exercised absolute veto over Parliamentary Salaries Bill.
*Suspensive Veto:* President returns the Bill for reconsideration. If Parliament passes it again, assent is mandatory.
– *Not available in case of Money bill* because it is already brought into the Parliament after the prior recommendation of the President.
*Pocket Veto:* No action taken by President. (No time limit to act.)
— This veto was first used by *Gyani Zail Singh for Indian Post Office (amendment) bill 1986*.

items: Estimates of Expenditure are divided into two parts:

Only Lok Sabha has the right to vote on Demands for Grants. RS can only discuss it.

1. __**Consolidated Fund of India (Article 266(1))**__
All revenues received by Government of India
All loans raised
All money received in repayment of loans
Largest and most important fund
No money can be withdrawn without Parliamentary approval (Appropriation Act)
Covers: Salaries, schemes, defense, etc.
2. __**Public Account of India (Article 266(2))**__
Money held by government as a banker/trustee
Includes: Provident Fund, Small savings, Deposits, etc.
No Parliamentary approval required for withdrawal
Government only acts as custodian
3. __**Contingency Fund of India (Article 267)**__
Fund for emergency/unforeseen expenditure
Controlled by President of India
Operated by Finance Secretary on behalf of President
Requires post-facto approval of Parliament
The Appropriation Bill is introduced to authorize the withdrawal of money from the Consolidated Fund of India.
No amendments or voting on the Appropriation Bill amounts — it is passed as a formality after Demands for Grants are voted.
*Cut motions* are moved during the voting on Demands for Grants to show the disapproval towards the Govt.
1. *Policy Cut:* Reduce the amount to ₹1, to criticize government policy.
2. *Economy Cut:* Reduce by a specified amount, to suggest savings.
3. *Token Cut:* Reduce by ₹100, to raise a specific grievance.
*Supplementary grant:* granted when the amount authorised by Parliament is found insufficient
*Additional grant:* granted for additional expenditure upon some new service.
*Excess grant:* It is granted when money has been spent on any service in excess of the amount granted to that service in the budget.

blocks.text: **Article 200**: Assent to Bills by the Governor

items: Under Article 200 of the Indian Constitution, the Governor has the following four options when a bill is presented:
1. Assent to the Bill: The Governor may give assent, and the bill becomes an Act.
2. Withhold Assent: The Governor may refuse assent, and the bill fails.
3. Return the Bill (if not a Money Bill): The Governor may return the bill to the State Legislature for reconsideration. If passed again, the Governor is bound to give assent.
4. Reserve the Bill for the President: The Governor may reserve the bill for consideration of the President, especially in certain cases of constitutional importance.

Increased the size of the legislative councils, both Central and Provincial. The no. of members in the central legislative council was raised from 16 to 60.
Members : Official majority in central legislative council and non-official majority in Provincial legislative council.
It provided for the association of Indians with Executive Councils of the Viceroys and Governor (*Satyendra Prasad Sinha* was the *1st* Indian to join, later appointed as the Law member)
It *introduced Separate Electorate for Muslims* (System of Communal Representation) i.e. Muslim members were elected by muslim voters (*Lord Minto: Father of Communal Electorate*)
Members were allowed to ask supplementary questions, move resolutions on the budget and so on.
It also provided for the separate representation of presidency corporations, chambers of commerce, universities and zamindars.
Also known as *Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms* (Montagu- Secretary of State for India and Lord Chelmsford - Viceroy of India).
*Dyarchy introduced in provinces.*
Classified all the subjects of administration into the central subjects and the provincial subjects.

blocks.text: Provincial subjects were further divided into **two parts: Transferred and Reserved Subjects.**

*Transferred subjects: (Governor-General not responsible)- Public health, Education, Local Self government, Agriculture, etc.*
*Reserved subjects: (Governor-General responsible)- Police Administration of justice, Prison, Land revenue, Finance, etc.*

items: It introduced Bicameralism and direct elections in the country for the 1st time consisting of an Upper House (Council of State) and a Lower House (Legislative Assembly).
It required that the 3 of the 6 members of the Viceroy's executive council (other than the Commander-in-Chief) were to be Indian.
It created a new office of the High Commissioner for India in London and transferred to him some of the functions.
Public Service Commission was established and a Central Public Service Commission was set up in 1926 for recruiting civil servants.
It separated Provincial budget from central budget.
It extended the system of *Separate Electorate for : Sikhs, Indian Christians, Anglo-Indians and Europeans.*
It *abolished dyarchy in the provinces* and *introduced dyarchy at the Centre,* Provided autonomy to the Provinces.
It provided for the establishment of an All India Federation consisting of provinces and princely states as units.
It *introduced bicameralism in 6 out of 11 provinces.* (Bengal, Bombay, Madras, Bihar, Assam and the United Provinces).
It reserved certain no. of seats for Scheduled Castes, women and labours.
It *extended adult franchise* - Only 14% of total population had the right to vote.
*Reserve Bank of India was established.*
Federal Public Service Commission, Provincial Public Service Commission and Joint Public Service Commission were established.
*Federal Court was setup in 1937.*
*Burma (now Myanmar) was separated from India.*

The Act divided the powers between the Centre and units in three lists-Federal List (for Centre, with 59 items), Provincial List (for provinces, with 54 items) and the Concurrent List (for both, with 36 items)
Residuary powers were given to the Viceroy (Governor-General)
It abolished the council of 15 members.
Two new provinces were created : Sindh and Orissa.
Appointment of an Auditor-general.
Establishment of Federal Railway authority.
It *ended the British rule in India* and declared India as an independent and sovereign state from August 15, 1947.
Partition of India and creation of two independent dominions of India and Pakistan with the right to secede from the British Commonwealth was done.
It *abolished the office of Viceroy* and provided a Governor General, who was to be appointed by the British King on the advice of the dominion cabinet.
It abolished the office of the Secretary of State for India and transferred his functions to the Secretary of State for Commonwealth Affairs.
It *proclaimed the lapse of British paramountcy* over the Indian princely states and treaty relations with tribal areas from August 15, 1947.
It granted freedom to the Indian princely states either to join the Dominion of India or Dominion of Pakistan or to remain independent.
The Governor-General would have full power to assent to any bill in the name of His Majesty.
It designated the Governor-General of India and the provincial governors as constitutional (nominal) heads of the states.
It dropped the title of Emperor of India from the royal titles of the King of England.
It discontinued the appointment to civil services and reservation of posts by the secretary of state for India.
It is also known as *the Partition plan/Mountbatten plan/June 3rd plan.*

blocks.text: *At the stroke of midnight of 14-15 August, 1947, the British rule came to an end.*

*1st Governor General of Independent India : Mountbatten*
*1st and last Indian Governor General: C Rajagopalachari*

So now we have seen that our Indian Constitution is a unique and comprehensive document drawing upon the best practices from a variety of foreign constitutions to suit India's diverse needs. Here is a summarised culmination of all the sources of our constitution:

blocks.caption: Mind-map: SOURCES OF INDIAN CONSTITUTION — Features Borrowed from other Nations (central node with Ashoka Chakra), arrows radiating to each source country/act. Rows transcribed in the table below.

rows: Procedure for amendment of the Constitution; Election of Rajya Sabha members using proportional representation by single transferable vote.

Parliamentary system of government; Bicameral legislature (Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha); Rule of Law; Legislative procedure; Single citizenship; Cabinet system; Concept of the Speaker and his role; Parliamentary privileges; Prerogative writs (like habeas corpus, mandamus, etc.)
Emergency powers (suspension of Fundamental Rights); Provisions regarding proclamation and effects of Emergency.
Concept of Procedure established by law (as opposed to "due process of law" in the U.S.).

Fundamental Rights; Independence of the judiciary; Judicial review; Written Constitution.; Preamble; Office of President and Vice President; Impeachment procedure; Removal of Supreme Court and High Court Judges; Separation of powers among the three organs.
Concurrent List; Freedom of trade, commerce, and intercourse within the country; Joint sitting of the two Houses of Parliament to resolve deadlock.
Republican form of government; Ideals of Liberty, Equality, and Fraternity (included in the Preamble).
Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP); Nomination of members to the Rajya Sabha by the President; Method of election of the President.
Federal structure with a strong Centre; Residuary powers vested in the Centre; Appointment of Governors by the Centre; Advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme Court; Quasi-federal nature of Constitution

Federal structure of government; Office of Governor; Public Service Commissions; Emergency provisions; Administrative details (civil services, police, etc.); Judiciary system; Provincial autonomy; Integrated Judiciary
Fundamental Duties; Ideals of justice (social, economic, and political) in the Preamble.

items: The Age of Consent Act 1891 raised the minimum marital age for girls from 10 to 12 years.
Manipur was the first in independent India to hold elections based on Universal Adult Franchise.
40th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976: It brought India's maritime zones (territorial waters, continental shelf, EEZ) under Parliament's jurisdiction by amending Article 297.
"Reward for Loyalty" in Ministerial Selection: It refers to the practice of appointing ministers as a recognition of their long-standing support, commitment and loyalty to the party or leadership.
Sikkim has the smallest legislative assembly (Vidhan Sabha) in India with only 32 members.

On 9 August 2021, the Parliament passed the Limited Liability Partnership (Amendment) Bill, 2021, amending the Limited Liability Partnership Act, 2008 to promote ease of doing business and decriminalize certain offences.
Article 371A (Special provisions for Nagaland) was inserted in the Constitution of India by the 13th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1962.

'Legal Positivism Theory' was given by - John Austin, It states that law is a set of rules created and enforced by the state and the validity of law does not depend on morality of ethics (defined law as the command of the sovereign).
Most cabinet committees are headed by the Prime Minister, except cabinet committee on parliamentary affairs which is usually headed by the Minister of Defence.

blocks.text: The National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (NCDRC) currently operates under the Consumer Protection Act, 2019, which replaced the previous Consumer Protection Act of 1986.
